title: Silk Fibroin Microneedle Patches for Therapeutic Delivery
abstract: Novel pharmaceutical drugs – so called biologics - constitute a new generation of therapeutics that are based on biomolecules or (stem) cells. Their storage, distribution and application require novel delivery methods, giving that biologics are fragile and unable to pass epidermal layers. The objective of this proposal is to develop silk fibroin microneedle (MN) patches for therapeutic delivery. This innovative approach will enable the administration of drugs, particularly unconventional payloads such as biologics, which pose challenges for conventional administration methods. Currently, the few existing silk microneedles grossly underutilize silk fibroin’s potential to be chemically modified to produce bio- and cytocompatible materials with highly tunable properties. We will develop and validate materials and chemical configurations of silk MNs to enable the transdermal delivery of a broader collection of therapeutic payloads than what current MNs can accommodate. We will generate reproducible methods, harnessing silk’s enormous potential as a biomaterial for medical devices, and greatly improving replicability and control over the resulting constructs. In conjunction with our industrial partner, Hyacinth Proteins, we aim to develop market-ready MN patches, who will be engineered to administer antifreeze proteins and bioactive proteins from milk, with a therapeutic focus to mitigate frostbite and chronic wounds. Notably, there are currently no pharmaceutical remedies available for these conditions on the market. We endeavor to create a competitive product by capitalizing on our readily access to bioactive proteins, our proficiency in testing and validating silk material configurations, and our bioanalytical expertise in assessing functionalities.
